Curriculum → Case studies and interview drills → Case study: sales rank by category

Lesson 9 of 9

Case study: sales rank by category

Compute and serve the top-selling items by category using transactional sales logs, rolling time windows, batch aggregation, and very hot read traffic.

30-40 min Case studies and interview drills 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

This drill is great for discussing log ingestion, windowed aggregation, batch versus stream updates, and the split between analytical compute and read APIs.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Raw events and ranking windows Capture product, category, quantity, and timestamp in append-only logs2. Aggregation pipeline Aggregate to per-category totals in offline or nearline jobs3. Serving and freshness trade-offs Cache the most-requested category rankings

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing This drill is great for discussing log ingestion, windowed aggregation, batch versus stream updates, and the split between analytical compute and read APIs. 2. Raw events and ranking windows Capture product, category, quantity, and timestamp in append-only logs3. Aggregation pipeline Aggregate to per-category totals in offline or nearline jobs4. Serving and freshness trade-offs Cache the most-requested category rankings
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

This drill is great for discussing log ingestion, windowed aggregation, batch versus stream updates, and the split between analytical compute and read APIs.

What to say first

  • Separate raw transaction ingest from the ranking read path.
  • Explain the rolling window and aggregation cadence clearly.
  • Store precomputed ranking outputs in a serving-friendly shape.
2

Study section

Raw events and ranking windows

Sales rank starts from transactional events. The ranking service should aggregate the last week's data by category and product without overloading the serving database.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Capture product, category, quantity, and timestamp in append-only logs
  • Treat category membership as reference data that shapes the aggregation key
  • Recompute or incrementally update rankings on a clear cadence
3

Study section

Aggregation pipeline

Batch systems such as MapReduce fit the original primer solution, but the core interview signal is choosing an aggregation pipeline that keeps serving traffic isolated from heavy compute.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Aggregate to per-category totals in offline or nearline jobs
  • Write the ranked results into a compact serving table or cache
  • Backfill or rerun jobs when upstream logs arrive late or are repaired
4

Study section

Serving and freshness trade-offs

Reads are extremely hot, so the serving layer should return small ranked lists from cache or precomputed tables while making freshness guarantees explicit.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Cache the most-requested category rankings
  • Decide whether hourly, near-real-time, or daily freshness is required
  • Observe ranking lag, cache hit rate, and job completion health

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Separate raw transaction ingest from the ranking read path.
  • Explain the rolling window and aggregation cadence clearly.
  • Store precomputed ranking outputs in a serving-friendly shape.
  • Talk about late data, backfills, and ranking freshness.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Computing rankings directly from the primary transaction database on every read.
  • Ignoring how products in multiple categories change the aggregation key.
  • Promising real-time freshness without discussing the cost.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • Would you use batch, stream, or hybrid aggregation for sales rank?
  • How do you serve a top-products page at very high read volume?
  • What happens when late or corrected transactions arrive?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Back-of-the-envelope estimation Foundations and estimationRelational data modeling and indexing Data storage and indexingCaching layers and cache placement Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: sales rank by category

Take the product shape from the case study and test one read path plus one write or ingestion path under pressure. Apply it to case study: sales rank by category so the trade-offs feel concrete.

7 nodes · 6 links

Browser · Native flow-graph compilerWASM · Pyodide / Python WASM adapterWASM · Lua / custom WASM adapter

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 1: edge · 90000 rps · 4 ms
  • Line 2: service · 26000 rps · 12 ms
  • Line 3: cache · 110000 rps · 3 ms
  • Line 4: database · 8500 rps · 20 ms
  • Line 5: database · 18000 rps · 18 ms
  • Line 6: queue · 32000 rps · 10 ms

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Interview opening

0 headings · 0 bullets · 0 words

Diagnostics

Things to fix or tighten

  • Line 1: Start by adding at least one heading so the answer has a visible structure.

Interview opening

You are starting a mock interview for "Case study: sales rank by category". Capture the product assumptions, scale estimates, core APIs, and the first diagram you would draw.

Goal

Frame the problem, scope, assumptions, and success metrics before you draw components.

Guardrails

  • Would you use batch, stream, or hybrid aggregation for sales rank?
  • Raw events and ranking windows
  • Aggregation pipeline
  • Separate raw transaction ingest from the ranking read path.

Suggested Structure

  • Requirements and assumptions
  • Scale estimate
  • Core APIs
  • First diagram
